content.text: In this photo I snapped, I’ve packed baby carrots, celery hearts, peanuts, dried berries, and blood orange slices in snack tins. Could you explain the best way to set up compartments and pairings to keep each component fresh and crisp, avoid moisture transfer, and optimize steady energy release?

conversations.content:
Absolutely—let’s break this down step by step.

1. Compartmentalize to Control Moisture  
   • Separate wet from dry: Store citrus slices (blood orange) in their own sealed compartment or small cup within the tin. The same goes for any dips or spreads—keep them isolated so juices don’t migrate onto nuts or veggies, which would make them soggy.  
   • Use breathable dividers: Silicone or bamboo dividers allow airflow while keeping items distinct. If you can’t find dividers, small silicone baking cups or reusable mini containers work just as well.

2. Maintain Veggie Crispness  
   • Pre-soak and pat dry: For celery and carrots, a quick dip in cold water for 10–15 minutes before packing helps them stay firm. Dry them thoroughly and wrap them in a barely damp paper towel before placing them in their compartment—this balances hydration without excess moisture.  
   • Airtight seal: Use a tight-closing lid or silicone gasket to prevent air exchange that can lead to wilting.

3. Preserve Nutty Freshness  
   • Oil separation: Peanuts and other nuts naturally release oils. Keep them in a compartment lined with a small parchment paper square so any excess oil doesn’t seep into adjacent foods. This also helps prevent oily residue from clinging to the tin.  
   • Cool, dark storage: If you’re not eating right away, store your tin in a cool spot or insulated lunch bag—heat accelerates rancidity.

4. Protect Dried Fruit Texture  
   • Humidity control: Dried berries can reabsorb moisture from the air. Tuck a small food-safe desiccant packet (silica-gel) or a folded paper towel in their compartment to absorb stray humidity.  
   • Keep away from citrus: Since dried berries will soften with contact, ensure they’re in a dry, separate cup.

5. Optimize Nutrient Synergy for Steady Energy  
   • Protein + fat + fiber: Peanuts bring protein and healthy fats; carrots and celery supply fiber and water; dried fruit and blood orange deliver quick carbohydrates plus vitamin C. Eating these in tandem moderates blood sugar impact—start with a wedge of orange (to enhance iron absorption), follow with a few nuts (to slow digestion), then finish with crunchy veggies for sustained fullness.  
   • Staggered snacking: Instead of munching everything at once, try nibbling a few items every 30–45 minutes. That keeps your energy levels balanced without a single big sugar spike.

6. Packing Tips for On-the-Go Convenience  
   • Bento-style container: A 4-section bento box is ideal—you’ll have one well-fitting chamber for each item.  
   • Label compartments: If you prep multiple tins in advance, a quick sticky-note on top can remind you of the ideal eating order.  
   • Keep utensils separate: If you plan to use a dip or eat the orange slices neatly, add a folded piece of wax paper or a small fork in a side pocket of your bag.

By using separate, airtight compartments and balancing your macros—protein from peanuts, fiber from vegetables, and vitamin-rich fruit—you’ll preserve texture, prevent moisture crossover, and enjoy a steady stream of energy throughout your day.

content.text: In this photo I snapped of my pineapple upside-down cake, the pineapple chunks have a deep, glossy caramel sheen but the cake still looks perfectly moist. What techniques did I use to achieve that balance of crisp, sticky fruit topping and a tender crumb underneath?

conversations.content:
You’re describing a classic caramel–fruit interface that’s the hallmark of a well-executed pineapple upside-down cake. Achieving that vibrant, sticky pineapple topping without turning the cake into a soggy mess involves a few key steps:

1. Caramelizing the Sugar and Butter
   • Ratio and Timing: Start by melting an even ratio of granulated sugar and unsalted butter—typically 1/2 cup each—over medium heat until the sugar dissolves and the mixture just begins to darken. Watch closely: you want a rich amber color, not burnt brown. This develops flavor without bitterness.
   • Temperature Control: Keep the heat moderate (around 320–340°F on a candy thermometer) so the sugar and butter blend slowly and evenly. Rapid heating fosters uneven caramelization and can separate the butter fat from the sugar.

2. Layering the Pineapples and Caramel
   • Even Spacing: Arrange fresh or canned pineapple rings (or bite-sized chunks, as in your photo) in a single layer directly on top of the hot caramel. Press them gently so they sink just below the surface but don’t get completely submerged. This helps them hold their shape when inverted.
   • Reserve Juices: If you’re using canned pineapple, drain and reserve the syrup. You can fold a tablespoon or two into your cake batter to enhance moisture without inundating the caramel layer.

3. Crafting the Batter
   • Sturdy Yet Tender: Use an all-purpose flour cake batter enriched with sour cream or yogurt (about ¼ cup) for moisture. A chemical leavener (baking powder and a pinch of baking soda) gives lift without creating large air pockets that might break the fruit layer.
   • Incorporating Fat: Add melted butter (or a combination of butter and neutral oil) to the batter for richness. This extra fat helps maintain a tender crumb even under the weight of the caramelized fruit.

4. Baking and Inverting
   • Moderate Oven Temperature: Bake at around 350°F for 35–40 minutes. That temperature is hot enough to set the batter quickly around the fruit but slow enough to let the caramel bubble and thicken.
   • Timed Inversion: Remove the cake from the oven and let it rest for just 5–10 minutes on a cooling rack—no longer. Waiting too long allows steam to saturate the fruit topping and soak into the cake. When it’s still warm but not piping hot, invert it onto your serving plate in one swift motion.

5. Finishing Touches
   • Warm It Up: If the caramel seems too firm, briefly warm the bottom of the inverted cake with a blow torch or place the plate on a warm pan to loosen it.
   • Gloss Boost: For extra shine, brush the fruit with a thin glaze made from warmed apricot jam strained of any solids. This step isn’t strictly necessary, but it heightens that mirror-like finish.

By carefully balancing caramel temperature, layering technique, batter composition, and inversion timing, you preserve the pineapple’s glossy, slightly crisp coating while the cake beneath remains soft and moist. The result is exactly what you captured in your photo—a stunning, flavor-packed dessert with just the right structure and shine.

content.text: Thanks, that’s really helpful! I’m curious about the step where you reserve some pineapple syrup for the batter. How exactly should I incorporate that syrup without making the batter too wet, and are there any other tricks to balance moisture in the cake?

conversations.content:
Great question! Incorporating pineapple syrup can boost flavor and moisture, but you do need to balance liquids and dry ingredients:

1. Measure Carefully: Start by measuring the syrup by volume—use no more than 2 tablespoons for a standard 9-inch cake batter. If you want more pineapple flavor, reduce the recipe’s other liquid (milk or sour cream) by the same amount.

2. Adjust Dry Ingredients: If you add syrup beyond a tablespoon or two, increase the flour by about 1 to 2 tablespoons to maintain batter structure. This prevents it from becoming too loose and risking a dense crumb.

3. Gentle Folding: Fold the syrup in at the end of the mixing process, just until streaks disappear. Overmixing can develop gluten and make the cake tough, plus it can encourage the syrup to sink to the bottom.

4. Monitor Consistency: Aim for a batter that’s thick enough to hold a spoonful on its side without sliding. If it looks runny, whisk in another tablespoon of flour or a teaspoon of cornstarch.

5. Alternative Moisture Tricks:
   • Yogurt or Buttermilk: Swap half the sour cream for Greek yogurt or buttermilk to add tang and moisture without extra sweetness.
   • Brown Sugar: Replace up to 1/4 cup of granulated sugar with light brown sugar in the batter for a deeper, caramel-like flavor that complements the pineapple.

By making small adjustments—measuring syrup, cutting back on other liquids, and tweaking your flour—you’ll keep the batter balanced. The result: an evenly moist crumb that won’t wilt under the caramelized fruit topping.

content.text: In this photo I took at my favorite fast-food spot, the fries are shoestring cut and look ultra-crispy. What cooking methods—like soaking or double-frying—create that texture, and how does the thickness of the cut affect the final result?

conversations.content:
Great question! Those thin, ultra-crispy shoestring fries rely on a combination of cut size, starch management, and a two-stage frying process. Here’s a step-by-step breakdown:

1. Selecting the Potato and Cut Thickness
   • Variety: Starchy potatoes (like Russets) yield a fluffier interior once cooked. Their lower moisture content helps the exterior stay crisp.  
   • Cut Size: Shoestring fries (around 3 – 4 mm thick) have a large surface-to-volume ratio, which means more of the potato is exposed to hot oil. This encourages rapid moisture evaporation, giving you that hallmark crisp edge. Thicker cuts, by contrast, will stay softer inside but won’t reach that same level of crunch.

2. Soaking to Remove Surface Starch
   • Why soak? Fresh-cut potatoes are coated in surface starch, which can cause fries to clump and caramelize too quickly.  
   • Method: Submerge your cut fries in cold water for at least 30 minutes (or even up to 2 hours). This step washes away excess starch and prevents the exterior from becoming gummy.  
   • Drying: After soaking, drain and pat the fries completely dry with kitchen towels. Any lingering moisture will cause dangerous oil splatter and inhibit crisping.

3. Blanching (First Fry)
   • Temperature & Time: Heat your oil to a lower temperature—around 275°F (135°C)—and fry the dried potatoes in small batches for 3–5 minutes. You’re not looking for color here, just gently cooking the interior.  
   • Purpose: This stage gelatinizes the potato’s starches and sets the structure, so the inside becomes tender without browning the outside.
   • Rest: Remove the fries and drain them on a rack or paper towels. Let them cool completely—if you have time, refrigerate or briefly blast-freeze them. This crystallizes residual moisture, further improving the crisp factor.

4. Final Fry (Second Fry)
   • Temperature & Time: Raise your oil to 375°F (190°C). Return the pre-blanched fries in small batches for 1–2 minutes until they turn golden brown and crackle on the surface.  
   • Outcome: The rapid second fry dehydrates the outer layer, creating a thin, shatter-crisp shell while preserving the soft interior.

5. Seasoning and Serving
   • Immediately after the second fry, season with fine salt or your favorite spice blend. Because the fries are still hot and lightly oily, the seasoning adheres well.
   • Serve hot—shoestring fries lose their peak crispness quickly, so enjoy them straight from the fryer.

Key Takeaways on Thickness:
• Thinner fries = more surface contact with oil = faster moisture loss = crispier finish.  
• Thicker cuts hold more interior moisture and develop a fluffier core but sacrifice some crunch.  
• For the shoestring look and texture, aim for 3 – 4 mm thickness, follow the soak/blanch/double-fry routine, and keep your oil temperatures precise.

With this approach, you can replicate that signature ultra-crispy, shoestring style at home—just like the ones in your photo!

content.text: In this photo I snapped of my breakfast—black coffee and a bacon-topped donut—can you explain how the key flavor compounds in the bacon, the sweet glaze and the coffee interact on the palate? What adjustments could I make to either the donut recipe or the coffee roast to really play up that sweet-salty-bitter balance?

conversations.content:
Great question! When you combine a bacon-topped donut and black coffee, you’re engaging several flavor dimensions all at once:

1. Maillard Aromas from Bacon and Donut
 • Bacon’s browning process (Maillard reaction) creates savory, smoky, and slightly toasty notes.  
 • The donut’s exterior, especially if it’s fried just until golden, also develops mild toasty and caramelized sugars.  
Together, these aromas reinforce each other, giving you a warm, rounded base of scent and taste.

2. Sweetness and Salt Synergy
 • Salt in the bacon intensifies your perception of sweetness in the donut’s glaze by elevating sugar receptors—this is the classic sweet-and-salty interplay.  
 • If the glaze uses a high ratio of simple sugars (like a plain sugar or honey glaze), the salt will make it pop, but can also feel one-dimensional if over-done.

3. Coffee Bitterness and Acidity
 • Black coffee delivers bitterness from chlorogenic acids and roasted phenolic compounds.  
 • Its acidity cuts through the donut’s fat and sugar, cleansing the palate between bites.  
 • A darker roast will be fuller and more bitter, while a lighter roast tends toward bright acidity and floral or fruity notes.

Tweak Suggestions:
• Adjust the Donut Glaze:  
 – Swap in a brown-butter or maple glaze to introduce nutty or caramel undertones that harmonize with bacon’s smoke.  
 – Sprinkle a pinch of flaky sea salt on top rather than relying solely on bacon salt—this gives bursts of salt without overwhelming sweetness.

• Refine the Bacon:  
 – Lightly candy the bacon in a brown sugar and black pepper cure so you get an extra layer of sweet spice.  
 – Pat the cooked bacon dry and bake it briefly to remove excess grease, keeping the bite crisp and flavor-forward.

• Choose Your Coffee Roast:  
 – Medium roasts with chocolatey, low-acid profiles provide a smooth bitterness that pairs elegantly with sweet donuts.  
 – If you prefer a brighter counterpoint, a light-to-medium roast with citrus or berry notes will cut through the donut’s richness in a refreshing way.

• Serving Temperature and Technique:  
 – Brew your coffee just off boil (around 200°F) to preserve nuanced aromatics.  
 – Allow the donut to cool slightly so the glaze isn’t too molten; that helps the bacon and sugar stay distinct on each bite.

Putting it all together, try using a maple-brown-butter glaze on your donut, top with a sprinkle of sea salt and crisp peppered bacon, then pair it with a medium-roast coffee showcasing chocolate and caramel notes. The result should be a layered tasting experience where sweet, salty, bitter and even a hint of acidity dance together bite after sip.

Dear Maggie,

I hope you're doing well! I'm reaching out because I've been thinking about how we can further streamline our processes while still ensuring the highest quality products at your bakery. Over the past few months, I've been working on new methods that modernize ingredient selection and recipe design, reducing waste and cutting down production time without compromising on taste. This approach not only supports efficient operations but also helps in controlling costs—a win-win for your business.

I believe that these modernized, efficient practices could integrate seamlessly into your current operations, helping you maintain your reputation for quality while also paving the way for innovative product development. I'd love to discuss how we can implement a few of these ideas to drive further success at Maggie's Artisan Bakery.

Looking forward to your thoughts.

Best regards,
Adam Burton

Can you explain what the key points of the email are?

Sure! The email from Adam Burton outlines a proposal to modernize some of your bakery's processes. Here are the key points:

1. Streamlined, modern methods: Adam is suggesting that by updating how ingredients are selected and how recipes are designed, you could make your production process more efficient. Although he doesn’t spell it out entirely, his emphasis on "modernizing" and "streamlined practices" hints at moving away from more traditional or time-consuming methods.

2. Efficiency and cost control: A significant focus of the email is on reducing waste and cutting down production time. This not only speeds up operations but also helps in managing costs—both of which can be important for reinvesting in growing your business.

3. Balancing quality with innovation: While he talks about improving efficiency, Adam makes sure to mention that these changes won't compromise the bakery's renowned quality. This balances the need for innovation with the core values you hold dear at Maggie's Artisan Bakery.

Overall, the email is a proposal to adopt some more efficient, modern techniques in your bakery operations, aligning with your drive for quality improvement and effective management of resources. It’s a subtle nudge towards evolving your processes while still keeping the artisanal essence you’re known for.

Thanks for breaking that down! I’m particularly interested in the part about modernizing ingredient selection and recipe design. Could you dive a bit deeper into what practical steps or techniques might be used in these areas for a small, artisanal bakery? How could these strategies be implemented without losing the unique, handmade touch that customers appreciate?

Absolutely, that’s a great follow-up question. For a small, artisanal bakery, blending modern efficiency with traditional charm can be achieved without sacrificing authenticity. Here are a few practical steps and techniques:

1. Digital Inventory and Supplier Integration: You might consider using simple inventory management software that keeps track of ingredients and predicts usage patterns. By integrating digitally with trusted suppliers, you can ensure that ingredient sourcing is both timely and cost-effective. This tech-driven solution doesn’t interfere with the manual crafting of recipes but rather supports it behind the scenes.

2. Recipe Optimization Tools: There are user-friendly recipe design and costing tools available that allow you to analyze ingredient combinations and quantities to minimize waste. These tools can suggest slight adjustments that improve consistency and efficiency in production, without altering the core characteristics of your unique recipes.

3. Batch Processing Enhancements: Modern kitchen equipment designed for small-scale production can speed up certain processes (like mixing or pre-measuring ingredients) while still leaving the artisanal touch in the final product assembly. This enhancement can help free up time for your skilled bakers to focus on creativity and quality.

4. Continuous Feedback and Iteration: Employing a system (even if it’s as simple as a digital log or shared spreadsheet) for tracking customer feedback related to new process changes can be highly beneficial. This can allow you to gradually iterate on your recipes and processes while keeping your customers’ experiences front and center.

By integrating these modern practices, you maintain control over the creative process while gaining efficiencies elsewhere in the operation. Essentially, it's about supporting the craft with smart tools rather than replacing the hands-on, artisanal expertise that your bakery is known for.

I hope you are doing well. I've been reflecting on our previous discussion and wanted to reach out with a few supportive words. I remember well how, in the face of tough challenges at that notable event last year, you managed to turn setbacks into stepping stones towards even greater innovation and quality. It’s clear that every experience has further refined your unique approach to bakery artistry. Your creative resilience has not only inspired me but has also been a beacon for many in our community. Please know that I am here to support you as you continue to grow and embrace new opportunities.

Warm regards,
Charles

"

Could you please help me explain what Emily (via Charles) might be implicitly addressing in her message? I’d like to better understand the underlying sentiments without directly focusing on any specific past events.

Sure, here’s an explanation of the email:

In this note, the sender subtly acknowledges a challenging experience you encountered in a past high-profile event by referring to it indirectly as a "tough challenge". Instead of dwelling solely on that setback, the message emphasizes how you transformed it into an opportunity for innovation and growth. The writer admires your resilience and how you refined your creative process through that experience, which is highlighted as a turning point in your approach to your craft.

The use of phrases like "turned setbacks into stepping stones" and "refined your unique approach" suggests that this email is meant to provide encouragement, support, and validation. It implicitly recognizes that even though there were difficult moments, those experiences have contributed positively to your professional journey and the brand’s identity.

Overall, the message is both personal and professional, aiming to reinforce your confidence and reassure you that your efforts in overcoming past challenges are noticed and appreciated. It also subtly opens the door for continued collaboration and support as you move forward with new opportunities.

Thanks for that detailed breakdown. I noticed that the email uses a series of indirect expressions to refer to past challenges without naming them specifically. Could you elaborate on how the choice of such indirect language might serve to both acknowledge difficulties and simultaneously uplift my confidence in moving forward?

Absolutely. The indirect language serves a dual purpose. On one side, by mentioning difficulties indirectly (using phrases like "tough challenges" or "setbacks"), it offers a respectful nod to past experiences without rehashing painful details. This subtle approach avoids triggering negative emotions while still recognizing the significance of those events in your journey.

On the other hand, this language is coupled with uplifting and empowering metaphors—such as turning setbacks into stepping stones—which celebrates your capability to transform hardship into growth and innovation. The balance in the message allows the sender to validate your past struggles and achievements while encouraging you to keep striving forward, thereby reinforcing your self-confidence and resilience in a gentle yet impactful manner.

1. Rice Flour: Widely used in gluten-free recipes, rice flour provides a light texture. Its neutral flavor makes it a good base for many baked goods, though you often need to blend it with other flours to achieve the structure usually provided by gluten.

2. Almond Flour: A popular choice for adding moisture and richness to baked goods, almond flour can lend a delicate texture, though it behaves differently, requiring adjustment in liquid ratios and sometimes additional binding agents.

3. Buckwheat Flour: Despite its name, buckwheat is gluten-free and adds a robust, earthy flavor, which can be quite complementary in artisanal breads and pastries. It often pairs well with lighter flours to balance texture and flavor.

4. Sorghum and Teff Flour: These flours have a slightly nutty taste and can work well when blended with other gluten-free flours. Teff, particularly, is known for its fine texture, making it suitable for delicate pastries.

5. Blending and Binders: Since gluten provides elasticity and structure, a blend of these flours is usually necessary. You might also consider adding binders like xanthan or guar gum, which can help mimic the texture of gluten-infused products without triggering sensitivities.

Understanding these alternatives from a scientific viewpoint can help you fine-tune recipes to suit not only the sensitive palate but also retain the quality and artisan spirit of your bakery. Adjustments might require some trial and error in balancing hydration levels, flavors, and texture, so experimenting with small batches before rolling out any broader menu changes is advisable.

Overall, by understanding the role of gluten and its alternatives at both a molecular and culinary level, you can better manage your bakery’s menu to cater to sensitive lifestyles while still keeping the quality artisanal experience your customers love.

conversations.content: Can you explain precisely how gluten sensitivity leads to discomfort and what steps I can take in my recipes to reduce its effects?

Certainly, Maggie. Gluten sensitivity, unlike celiac disease, doesn’t always show the same clear-cut immune response or intestinal damage, but it can still trigger significant discomfort and a range of symptoms. Here’s a detailed explanation:

1. Understanding Gluten Sensitive Responses: Gluten is a protein found in wheat, barley, and rye. For those with gluten sensitivity, ingestion of gluten can lead to discomfort such as bloating, abdominal pain, and fatigue, even if there is no immune-mediated damage like that seen in celiac disease. The exact mechanism isn’t completely understood, but some experts suggest that gluten may cause an innate immune response or alter the gut microbiome, leading to inflammation and impaired digestion.

2. The Biochemistry Behind It: When gluten is consumed, it is normally broken down by enzymes in the digestive tract. However, for individuals with gluten sensitivity, these enzymes may not work effectively, or the peptide fragments of gluten might be recognized by the body as irritants. This can trigger a low-grade inflammatory response, leading to symptoms without the severe intestinal damage seen in celiac disease.

3. Recipe Adjustments in a Bakery Setting: Given your focus on artisanal excellence, it’s crucial to maintain the quality and flavor in your gluten-free offerings. Consider the following steps:
   - Experiment with alternative flours: Options like rice, almond, buckwheat, or sorghum flour can provide texture and flavor while eliminating gluten. Each flour has its own unique characteristics and may require adjustments in hydration and mixing times.
   - Use binding agents: Gluten provides structure in baked goods. To replicate this in gluten-free recipes, you can incorporate ingredients like xanthan gum, guar gum, or psyllium husk. These help mimic the elasticity and structure that gluten typically provides.
   - Test and refine: Given your artisanal approach, it’s important to run small batch tests to fine-tune the ratios. Subtle differences in moisture content and mixing techniques can have a significant impact on the final product's quality.
   - Educate your customers: Consider adding an explanation on your menu or in your shop about gluten sensitivity, subtly emphasizing your commitment to quality ingredients that cater to diverse dietary needs. This not only ensures transparency but also reinforces your bakery’s thoughtful and community-centered approach.

4. Navigating Customer Queries: Your customers may have differing levels of understanding about gluten sensitivity. Offer clear and balanced explanations about why certain ingredients are used. Highlight safety and quality as core aspects of your bakery’s philosophy. Invite feedback to continually refine these offerings, reinforcing trust and community engagement.

By adopting these strategies, you ensure that your menu adaptations maintain the culinary excellence your bakery is known for while being considerate of the sensitivities among your patrons. If you need further technical details or assistance with specific formulations, I’d be happy to help!

Thank you for the comprehensive explanation. I’m particularly intrigued by the binding agents you mentioned. Could you elaborate more on choosing between xanthan gum, guar gum, and psyllium husk? For example, how might the ratios differ in a gluten-free bread recipe to optimize the texture and moisture?

Great question! The choice of binding agents can really impact the final product, especially when replicating the structure that gluten usually provides in bread. Here are some specifics:

1. Xanthan Gum: This is one of the most popular binding agents in gluten-free baking. Typically, it’s used at about 1/2 teaspoon per cup of gluten-free flour. Xanthan gum helps to create elasticity and improves the dough’s ability to retain gases during rising. However, too much can make the bread overly gummy. It tends to be easier to incorporate, especially in recipes that are more moisture-intensive.

2. Guar Gum: Similar to xanthan, guar gum is used to enhance texture and hold moisture. The ratio for guar gum is usually slightly higher than that for xanthan gum—around 3/4 teaspoon per cup of gluten-free flour. Guar gum can produce a lighter crumb in some cases, but its performance might vary depending on the other ingredients in the recipe.

3. Psyllium Husk: This ingredient is a bit different, as it not only acts as a binding agent but also contributes to fiber content. It’s excellent for gluten-free bread because it forms a gel-like consistency when mixed with water, providing a texture that closely mimics gluten. Typically, you’d use about 1 to 1.5 teaspoons per cup of gluten-free flour. You may need to adjust the liquid in your recipe since psyllium absorbs a lot of water. It’s particularly useful when you want a slightly denser bread with a good rise and chewy texture.

The best approach is to start with these guidelines and then test small batches. Remember that interactions with other ingredients, especially different gluten-free flours (like rice, buckwheat, or sorghum), may necessitate tweaks. Often, a combination might yield the best results. If you’re targeting a lighter, airier texture, xanthan or guar gum might be preferable, while for a chewier, more fibrous outcome, psyllium husk is excellent. I recommend keeping notes on each batch to refine the formula further.
